findings.description: Off-by-one: range(total_chunks - 1) skips the final chunk (index total_chunks-1), so every assembled multi-chunk file is silently truncated/corrupt despite is_complete requiring all chunks; should be range(total_chunks).

Filename-collision/overwrite: dest_path is built from client filename and opened 'wb', so two sessions with the same filename silently overwrite each other and concurrent assembles interleave/corrupt; no uniqueness or locking.

Advertised 'verify' step is never enforced: assemble stores the file without calling verify_checksum or file_size_ok, and deletes the session (line 70) before any verification could run, so MAX_FILE_MB and the checksum are dead/unenforced in the handler's own flow.

No aggregate size enforcement during receive_chunk: chunks are written to disk with no cumulative cap, so MAX_FILE_MB is bypassable and a client can exhaust disk; file_size_ok runs only post-hoc and is never invoked by the handler.

total_chunks has no upper bound and sessions have no TTL/eviction; an absurd or never-completed total_chunks leaves the UploadSession in self._sessions forever, so _sessions grows unbounded (memory leak) and orphaned chunk files accumulate.

No error handling around file I/O (open/write at 49, 64, 67, 82): disk-full or permission errors raise uncaught and leave a partial dest_path / partial chunk on disk with no rollback or session state correction.

Path traversal: session.filename is taken from the client and only its extension is validated; os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, filename) with '../' or an absolute path escapes UPLOAD_DIR and writes attacker-controlled content to arbitrary locations (deferred to critic-security).
verify_checksum does f.read() of the whole file into memory; with a 200 MB cap this is a ~200 MB allocation per call instead of streaming in fixed-size blocks (deferred to critic-performance).

assemble is non-atomic: it writes the file, then cleans chunks, then deletes the session with no locking, so concurrent calls for the same upload_id can double-write/double-cleanup; only the re-entrant guard at line 61 prevents a hard KeyError on the second pass.
